records.question: Which player has participated in the highest number of winning matches and which player has participated in the highest number of losing matches, considering only matches where they actually played (excluding null entries) and where their team won or lost (excluding draws)?
records.pred_sql:
-- Winning matches: player is on the winning team (home win: home_team_goal > away_team_goal; away win: away_team_goal > home_team_goal)
-- Losing matches: player is on the losing team (home loss: home_team_goal < away_team_goal; away loss: away_team_goal < home_team_goal)
-- Exclude draws (home_team_goal = away_team_goal)
-- Exclude null player entries (player_api_id IS NOT NULL)
-- Two separate queries unioned, each with GROUP BY player, ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC, LIMIT 1

SELECT player_name, 'most_wins' AS result_type
FROM (
    SELECT p.player_name, COUNT(*) AS cnt
    FROM Match m
    JOIN Player p ON p.player_api_id IN (
        m.home_player_1, m.home_player_2, m.home_player_3, m.home_player_4, m.home_player_5,
        m.home_player_6, m.home_player_7, m.home_player_8, m.home_player_9, m.home_player_10, m.home_player_11,
        m.away_player_1, m.away_player_2, m.away_player_3, m.away_player_4, m.away_player_5,
        m.away_player_6, m.away_player_7, m.away_player_8, m.away_player_9, m.away_player_10, m.away_player_11
    )
    WHERE m.home_team_goal > m.away_team_goal
      AND (
          (p.player_api_id IN (m.home_player_1, m.home_player_2, m.home_player_3, m.home_player_4, m.home_player_5,
                               m.home_player_6, m.home_player_7, m.home_player_8, m.home_player_9, m.home_player_10, m.home_player_11)
           AND m.home_team_goal > m.away_team_goal)
          OR
          (p.player_api_id IN (m.away_player_1, m.away_player_2, m.away_player_3, m.away_player_4, m.away_player_5,
                               m.away_player_6, m.away_player_7, m.away_player_8, m.away_player_9, m.away_player_10, m.away_player_11)
           AND m.away_team_goal > m.home_team_goal)
      )
      AND p.player_api_id IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY p.player_api_id
    ORDER BY cnt DESC
    LIMIT 1
)
UNION ALL
SELECT player_name, 'most_losses' AS result_type
FROM (
    SELECT p.player_name, COUNT(*) AS cnt
    FROM Match m
    JOIN Player p ON p.player_api_id IN (
        m.home_player_1, m.home_player_2, m.home_player_3, m.home_player_4, m.home_player_5,
        m.home_player_6, m.home_player_7, m.home_player_8, m.home_player_9, m.home_player_10, m.home_player_11,
        m.away_player_1, m.away_player_2, m.away_player_3, m.away_player_4, m.away_player_5,
        m.away_player_6, m.away_player_7, m.away_player_8, m.away_player_9, m.away_player_10, m.away_player_11
    )
    WHERE m.home_team_goal < m.away_team_goal
      AND (
          (p.player_api_id IN (m.home_player_1, m.home_player_2, m.home_player_3, m.home_player_4, m.home_player_5,
                               m.home_player_6, m.home_player_7, m.home_player_8, m.home_player_9, m.home_player_10, m.home_player_11)
           AND m.home_team_goal < m.away_team_goal)
          OR
          (p.player_api_id IN (m.away_player_1, m.away_player_2, m.away_player_3, m.away_player_4, m.away_player_5,
                               m.away_player_6, m.away_player_7, m.away_player_8, m.away_player_9, m.away_player_10, m.away_player_11)
           AND m.away_team_goal < m.home_team_goal)
      )
      AND p.player_api_id IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY p.player_api_id
    ORDER BY cnt DESC
    LIMIT 1
)

query_skeleton.notes: Two separate queries for most wins and most losses, each with GROUP BY player, ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC, LIMIT 1

records.question: For each IPL season, identify the top three batsmen with the highest total runs scored and the top three bowlers with the most wickets taken, excluding ‘run out’, ‘hit wicket’, and ‘retired hurt’ dismissals. In the event of ties in runs or wickets, break the tie using the smaller player ID. Then output these six players in matched positions—batsman 1 with bowler 1, batsman 2 with bowler 2, and batsman 3 with bowler 3—in ascending order of the season ID, along with each player’s total runs or wickets.
records.pred_sql:
-- Step 1: Compute total runs per batsman per season (using ball_by_ball and batsman_scored)
-- Step 2: Compute total wickets per bowler per season (excluding run out, hit wicket, retired hurt)
-- Step 3: Rank top 3 batsmen per season (tie-break by player_id)
-- Step 4: Rank top 3 bowlers per season (tie-break by player_id)
-- Step 5: Join ranked batsmen and bowlers by season and position (1-3)
-- Step 6: Output season, batsman name, runs, bowler name, wickets, ordered by season

WITH batsman_runs AS (
    SELECT 
        b.match_id,
        b.over_id,
        b.ball_id,
        b.innings_no,
        b.striker AS player_id,
        SUM(b.runs_scored) AS total_runs
    FROM batsman_scored b
    GROUP BY b.match_id, b.over_id, b.ball_id, b.innings_no, b.striker
),
season_batsman_runs AS (
    SELECT 
        m.season_id,
        br.player_id,
        SUM(br.total_runs) AS total_runs
    FROM batsman_runs br
    JOIN match m ON br.match_id = m.match_id
    GROUP BY m.season_id, br.player_id
),
ranked_batsmen AS (
    SELECT 
        season_id,
        player_id,
        total_runs,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY season_id ORDER BY total_runs DESC, player_id ASC) AS rn
    FROM season_batsman_runs
),
bowler_wickets AS (
    SELECT 
        wt.match_id,
        wt.over_id,
        wt.ball_id,
        wt.innings_no,
        wt.player_out AS player_id,
        COUNT(*) AS wickets
    FROM wicket_taken wt
    WHERE wt.kind_out NOT IN ('run out', 'hit wicket', 'retired hurt')
    GROUP BY wt.match_id, wt.over_id, wt.ball_id, wt.innings_no, wt.player_out
),
season_bowler_wickets AS (
    SELECT 
        m.season_id,
        bw.player_id,
        SUM(bw.wickets) AS total_wickets
    FROM bowler_wickets bw
    JOIN match m ON bw.match_id = m.match_id
    GROUP BY m.season_id, bw.player_id
),
ranked_bowlers AS (
    SELECT 
        season_id,
        player_id,
        total_wickets,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY season_id ORDER BY total_wickets DESC, player_id ASC) AS rn
    FROM season_bowler_wickets
)
SELECT 
    rb.season_id,
    p1.player_name AS batsman_name,
    rb.total_runs,
    p2.player_name AS bowler_name,
    rw.total_wickets
FROM ranked_batsmen rb
JOIN ranked_bowlers rw ON rb.season_id = rw.season_id AND rb.rn = rw.rn
JOIN player p1 ON rb.player_id = p1.player_id
JOIN player p2 ON rw.player_id = p2.player_id
WHERE rb.rn <= 3
ORDER BY rb.season_id ASC, rb.rn ASC

query_skeleton.notes: Need subqueries to rank top 3 batsmen and top 3 bowlers per season, then join them in matched positions. Group by season and player, order by season and rank, limit per season.

records.question: Find the IDs of players who scored the highest number of partnership runs for each match. The output should include the IDs of two players, each with their individual scores and the total partnership score. For each pair, the player with the higher individual score should be listed as player 1, and the player with the lower score as player 2. In cases where both players have the same score, the player with the higher ID should be player 1, and the player with the lower ID should be player 2. There can be multiple rows for a single match.
records.pred_sql:
WITH partnership_runs AS (
    SELECT 
        b.match_id,
        b.striker AS player1_id,
        b.non_striker AS player2_id,
        COUNT(*) AS partnership_total
    FROM ball_by_ball b
    GROUP BY b.match_id, b.striker, b.non_striker
),
player_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        p.player_id,
        p.player_name,
        COUNT(*) AS total_runs
    FROM player p
    JOIN ball_by_ball b ON p.player_id = b.striker
    GROUP BY p.player_id
),
max_partnership_per_match AS (
    SELECT 
        match_id,
        MAX(partnership_total) AS max_total
    FROM partnership_runs
    GROUP BY match_id
),
top_partnerships AS (
    SELECT 
        pr.match_id,
        pr.player1_id,
        pr.player2_id,
        pr.partnership_total
    FROM partnership_runs pr
    JOIN max_partnership_per_match mpm ON pr.match_id = mpm.match_id AND pr.partnership_total = mpm.max_total
),
player_individual_scores AS (
    SELECT 
        tp.match_id,
        tp.player1_id,
        tp.player2_id,
        tp.partnership_total,
        COALESCE(ps1.total_runs, 0) AS player1_score,
        COALESCE(ps2.total_runs, 0) AS player2_score
    FROM top_partnerships tp
    LEFT JOIN player_scores ps1 ON tp.player1_id = ps1.player_id
    LEFT JOIN player_scores ps2 ON tp.player2_id = ps2.player_id
)
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN pis.player1_score > pis.player2_score THEN pis.player1_id
        WHEN pis.player2_score > pis.player1_score THEN pis.player2_id
        WHEN pis.player1_id > pis.player2_id THEN pis.player1_id
        ELSE pis.player2_id
    END AS player1_id,
    CASE 
        WHEN pis.player1_score > pis.player2_score THEN pis.player2_id
        WHEN pis.player2_score > pis.player1_score THEN pis.player1_id
        WHEN pis.player1_id > pis.player2_id THEN pis.player2_id
        ELSE pis.player1_id
    END AS player2_id,
    CASE 
        WHEN pis.player1_score > pis.player2_score THEN pis.player1_score
        WHEN pis.player2_score > pis.player1_score THEN pis.player2_score
        WHEN pis.player1_id > pis.player2_id THEN pis.player1_score
        ELSE pis.player2_score
    END AS player1_individual_score,
    CASE 
        WHEN pis.player1_score > pis.player2_score THEN pis.player2_score
        WHEN pis.player2_score > pis.player1_score THEN pis.player1_score
        WHEN pis.player1_id > pis.player2_id THEN pis.player2_score
        ELSE pis.player1_score
    END AS player2_individual_score,
    pis.partnership_total
FROM player_individual_scores pis
ORDER BY pis.match_id, pis.partnership_total DESC

query_skeleton.notes: Need to compute partnership runs per pair per match, find max partnership per match, then join back to get pairs with that total, ordering by individual scores and IDs.

records.question: Calculate the duration of each track, classify them as short, medium, or long, output the minimum and maximum time for each kind (in minutes) and the total revenue for each category, group by the category.
records.pred_sql:
SELECT 
    CASE 
        WHEN (Track.Milliseconds / 60000.0) < 4 THEN 'short'
        WHEN (Track.Milliseconds / 60000.0) BETWEEN 4 AND 7 THEN 'medium'
        ELSE 'long'
    END AS category,
    MIN(Track.Milliseconds / 60000.0) AS min_minutes,
    MAX(Track.Milliseconds / 60000.0) AS max_minutes,
    SUM(InvoiceLine.UnitPrice * InvoiceLine.Quantity) AS total_revenue
FROM Track
JOIN InvoiceLine ON Track.TrackId = InvoiceLine.TrackId
JOIN Invoice ON InvoiceLine.InvoiceId = Invoice.InvoiceId
GROUP BY category
ORDER BY category

query_skeleton.notes: Group by category (short/medium/long) with MIN, MAX of duration in minutes and SUM of revenue

records.question: Using a Salary Dataset where the salary values need to be cleaned by removing non-numeric characters and converting them to a numeric type, write a detailed SQL query that identifies the top 5 companies by average salary in each of Mumbai, Pune, New Delhi, and Hyderabad, then compares each company’s average salary in those cities to the overall national average salary. The final result should display four columns: Location, Company Name, Average Salary in State, and Average Salary in Country, listing only the top 5 companies in each of the specified locations.
records.pred_sql:
WITH cleaned_salaries AS (
    SELECT 
        Location,
        CompanyName,
        CAST(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(REPLACE(Salary, '₹', ''), ',', ''), '/yr', ''), '/mo', '') AS REAL) AS salary_clean
    FROM SalaryDataset
    WHERE Location IN ('Mumbai', 'Pune', 'New Delhi', 'Hyderabad')
),
national_avg AS (
    SELECT AVG(salary_clean) AS avg_country
    FROM cleaned_salaries
),
city_company_avg AS (
    SELECT 
        Location,
        CompanyName,
        AVG(salary_clean) AS avg_salary_city
    FROM cleaned_salaries
    GROUP BY Location, CompanyName
),
ranked_companies AS (
    SELECT 
        c.Location,
        c.CompanyName,
        c.avg_salary_city,
        n.avg_country,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY c.Location ORDER BY c.avg_salary_city DESC) AS rn
    FROM city_company_avg c
    CROSS JOIN national_avg n
)
SELECT 
    Location,
    CompanyName AS "Company Name",
    ROUND(avg_salary_city, 4) AS "Average Salary in State",
    ROUND(avg_country, 4) AS "Average Salary in Country"
FROM ranked_companies
WHERE rn <= 5
ORDER BY Location, avg_salary_city DESC

query_skeleton.notes: Requires subquery for cleaning salary and computing national average, then per-city grouping with AVG, ordering by average salary per city, and limiting to top 5 per location.

records.question: Calculate the total number of wickets taken by each bowler (excluding run-outs and other dismissals not attributed to the bowler), their economy rate (total runs conceded divided by total overs bowled, considering only runs scored off the bat and ignoring any extra runs like wides and no-balls), their strike rate (average number of balls bowled per wicket taken), and their best bowling performance in a single match (the match with the most wickets taken by the bowler, formatted as "wickets-runs" where runs are the runs conceded excluding extras).

WITH bowler_match_stats AS (

        b.match_id,
        COUNT(w.player_out) AS wickets,
        SUM(bs.runs_scored) AS runs_conceded
    FROM ball_by_ball b
    LEFT JOIN wicket_taken w ON b.match_id = w.match_id
        AND b.over_id = w.over_id
        AND b.ball_id = w.ball_id
        AND b.innings_no = w.innings_no
        AND w.kind_out NOT IN ('run out', 'retired hurt', 'obstructing the field')
    LEFT JOIN batsman_scored bs ON b.match_id = bs.match_id
        AND b.over_id = bs.over_id
        AND b.ball_id = bs.ball_id
        AND b.innings_no = bs.innings_no
    GROUP BY b.bowler, b.match_id
),
-- Step 2: Per-bowler best performance (most wickets, then least runs)
bowler_best AS (
    SELECT
        bowler,
        wickets || '-' || runs_conceded AS best_bowling,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY bowler ORDER BY wickets DESC, runs_conceded ASC) AS rn
    FROM bowler_match_stats
    WHERE wickets > 0
),
-- Step 3: Per-bowler aggregates
bowler_aggregates AS (
    SELECT
        b.bowler,
        COUNT(DISTINCT b.match_id || '-' || b.over_id || '-' || b.innings_no) AS total_balls,
        SUM(CASE WHEN w.player_out IS NOT NULL AND w.kind_out NOT IN ('run out', 'retired hurt', 'obstructing the field') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS total_wickets,
        SUM(bs.runs_scored) AS total_runs_conceded
    FROM ball_by_ball b
    LEFT JOIN wicket_taken w ON b.match_id = w.match_id
        AND b.over_id = w.over_id
        AND b.ball_id = w.ball_id
        AND b.innings_no = w.innings_no
    LEFT JOIN batsman_scored bs ON b.match_id = bs.match_id
        AND b.over_id = bs.over_id
        AND b.ball_id = bs.ball_id
        AND b.innings_no = bs.innings_no
    GROUP BY b.bowler
)
-- Step 4: Final output
SELECT
    p.player_name,
    ba.total_wickets,
    ROUND(CAST(ba.total_runs_conceded AS REAL) / (CAST(ba.total_balls AS REAL) / 6.0), 4) AS economy_rate,
    ROUND(CAST(ba.total_balls AS REAL) / NULLIF(ba.total_wickets, 0), 4) AS strike_rate,
    COALESCE(bb.best_bowling, '0-0') AS best_bowling
FROM bowler_aggregates ba
JOIN player p ON ba.bowler = p.player_id
LEFT JOIN bowler_best bb ON ba.bowler = bb.bowler AND bb.rn = 1
ORDER BY p.player_name

query_skeleton.notes: Need subquery for best bowling performance per bowler (most wickets in a single match), plus per-bowler aggregates for total wickets, economy rate, and strike rate.

records.question: For each player, list their ID, name, their most frequent role across all matches, batting hand, bowling skill, total runs scored, total matches played, total times they were dismissed, batting average (total runs divided by total dismissals), highest score in a single match, the number of matches in which they scored at least 30 runs, at least 50 runs, and at least 100 runs, total balls faced in their career, strike rate (total runs divided by total balls faced, multiplied by 100), total wickets taken, economy rate (average runs conceded per over), and their best bowling performance in a single match (most wickets taken in a match, formatted as "wickets taken-runs given", where the best performance is the one with the most wickets, and if tied, the fewest runs conceded). Ignore the extra runs data.
records.pred_sql:
-- For each player, compute all requested statistics
WITH player_roles AS (
    -- Get each player's role per match and count occurrences
    SELECT 
        pm.player_id,
        pm.role,
        COUNT(*) AS role_count,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY pm.player_id ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC) AS rn
    FROM player_match pm
    GROUP BY pm.player_id, pm.role
),
most_frequent_role AS (
    SELECT player_id, role AS most_frequent_role
    FROM player_roles
    WHERE rn = 1
),
player_matches AS (
    -- Total matches played by each player
    SELECT player_id, COUNT(DISTINCT match_id) AS total_matches
    FROM player_match
    GROUP BY player_id
),
player_runs AS (
    -- Total runs scored by each player
    SELECT b.striker AS player_id, SUM(bs.runs_scored) AS total_runs
    FROM ball_by_ball b
    JOIN batsman_scored bs ON b.match_id = bs.match_id AND b.over_id = bs.over_id AND b.ball_id = bs.ball_id AND b.innings_no = bs.innings_no
    GROUP BY b.striker
),
player_dismissals AS (
    -- Total times dismissed (player_out)
    SELECT player_out AS player_id, COUNT(*) AS total_dismissals
    FROM wicket_taken
    GROUP BY player_out
),
highest_score AS (
    -- Highest score in a single match (sum of runs per match)
    SELECT 
        b.striker AS player_id,
        MAX(match_runs) AS highest_score
    FROM (
        SELECT b.striker, b.match_id, SUM(bs.runs_scored) AS match_runs
        FROM ball_by_ball b
        JOIN batsman_scored bs ON b.match_id = bs.match_id AND b.over_id = bs.over_id AND b.ball_id = bs.ball_id AND b.innings_no = bs.innings_no
        GROUP BY b.striker, b.match_id
    ) sub
    GROUP BY player_id
),
match_thresholds AS (
    -- Count matches with at least 30, 50, 100 runs
    SELECT 
        b.striker AS player_id,
        COUNT(CASE WHEN match_runs >= 30 THEN 1 END) AS matches_ge_30,
        COUNT(CASE WHEN match_runs >= 50 THEN 1 END) AS matches_ge_50,
        COUNT(CASE WHEN match_runs >= 100 THEN 1 END) AS matches_ge_100
    FROM (
        SELECT b.striker, b.match_id, SUM(bs.runs_scored) AS match_runs
        FROM ball_by_ball b
        JOIN batsman_scored bs ON b.match_id = bs.match_id AND b.over_id = bs.over_id AND b.ball_id = bs.ball_id AND b.innings_no = bs.innings_no
        GROUP BY b.striker, b.match_id
    ) sub
    GROUP BY player_id
),
balls_faced AS (
    -- Total balls faced (each ball where player is striker)
    SELECT striker AS player_id, COUNT(*) AS total_balls_faced
    FROM ball_by_ball
    GROUP BY striker
),
wickets_taken AS (
    -- Total wickets taken by each bowler
    SELECT bowler AS player_id, COUNT(*) AS total_wickets
    FROM wicket_taken
    GROUP BY bowler
),
economy_rate AS (
    -- Economy rate: runs conceded per over bowled
    SELECT 
        b.bowler AS player_id,
        CAST(SUM(bs.runs_scored) AS REAL) / (COUNT(DISTINCT b.match_id || '-' || b.over_id || '-' || b.innings_no) / 6.0) AS economy_rate
    FROM ball_by_ball b
    LEFT JOIN batsman_scored bs ON b.match_id = bs.match_id AND b.over_id = bs.over_id AND b.ball_id = bs.ball_id AND b.innings_no = bs.innings_no
    GROUP BY b.bowler
),
best_bowling AS (
    -- Best bowling performance in a single match (most wickets, fewest runs)
    SELECT 
        b.bowler AS player_id,
        match_id,
        COUNT(*) AS wickets,
        COALESCE(SUM(bs.runs_scored), 0) AS runs_given,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY b.bowler ORDER BY COUNT(*) DESC, COALESCE(SUM(bs.runs_scored), 0) ASC) AS rn
    FROM ball_by_ball b
    LEFT JOIN batsman_scored bs ON b.match_id = bs.match_id AND b.over_id = bs.over_id AND b.ball_id = bs.ball_id AND b.innings_no = bs.innings_no
    WHERE b.bowler IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY b.bowler, b.match_id
),
best_bowling_formatted AS (
    SELECT player_id, CAST(wickets AS TEXT) || '-' || CAST(runs_given AS TEXT) AS best_bowling_performance
    FROM best_bowling
    WHERE rn = 1
)
SELECT 
    p.player_id AS ID,
    p.player_name AS Name,
    COALESCE(mfr.most_frequent_role, 'Unknown') AS most_frequent_role,
    p.batting_hand,
    p.bowling_skill,
    COALESCE(pr.total_runs, 0) AS total_runs,
    COALESCE(pm.total_matches, 0) AS total_matches,
    COALESCE(pd.total_dismissals, 0) AS total_dismissals,
    CASE WHEN COALESCE(pd.total_dismissals, 0) > 0 
         THEN CAST(COALESCE(pr.total_runs, 0) AS REAL) / pd.total_dismissals 
         ELSE NULL 
    END AS batting_average,
    COALESCE(hs.highest_score, 0) AS highest_score,
    COALESCE(mt.matches_ge_30, 0) AS matches_ge_30,
    COALESCE(mt.matches_ge_50, 0) AS matches_ge_50,
    COALESCE(mt.matches_ge_100, 0) AS matches_ge_100,
    COALESCE(bf.total_balls_faced, 0) AS total_balls_faced,
    CASE WHEN COALESCE(bf.total_balls_faced, 0) > 0 
         THEN CAST(COALESCE(pr.total_runs, 0) AS REAL) / bf.total_balls_faced * 100.0 
         ELSE NULL 
    END AS strike_rate,
    COALESCE(wt.total_wickets, 0) AS total_wickets,
    er.economy_rate,
    COALESCE(bbf.best_bowling_performance, '0-0') AS best_bowling_performance
FROM player p
LEFT JOIN most_frequent_role mfr ON p.player_id = mfr.player_id
LEFT JOIN player_matches pm ON p.player_id = pm.player_id
LEFT JOIN player_runs pr ON p.player_id = pr.player_id
LEFT JOIN player_dismissals pd ON p.player_id = pd.player_id
LEFT JOIN highest_score hs ON p.player_id = hs.player_id
LEFT JOIN match_thresholds mt ON p.player_id = mt.player_id
LEFT JOIN balls_faced bf ON p.player_id = bf.player_id
LEFT JOIN wickets_taken wt ON p.player_id = wt.player_id
LEFT JOIN economy_rate er ON p.player_id = er.player_id
LEFT JOIN best_bowling_formatted bbf ON p.player_id = bbf.player_id
ORDER BY p.player_id

query_skeleton.notes: Per-player aggregation with multiple aggregate columns; best bowling performance requires subquery for per-match wickets and runs, then ranking per player.

records.question: Which problems exceed the total number of times they appear in the solution table when counting all occurrences, across steps 1, 2, and 3, where any non-"Stack" model's maximum test score is lower than the "Stack" model's test score for the same step and version?
records.pred_sql:
SELECT ms.name
FROM model_score ms
WHERE ms.step IN (1, 2, 3)
  AND ms.model = 'Stack'
  AND ms.test_score > (
    SELECT MAX(ms2.test_score)
    FROM model_score ms2
    WHERE ms2.step = ms.step
      AND ms2.version = ms.version
      AND ms2.model != 'Stack'
  )
GROUP BY ms.name
HAVING COUNT(*) > (
  SELECT COUNT(*)
  FROM solution s
  WHERE s.name = ms.name
)

query_skeleton.notes: Need to compare per-problem count in solution vs total occurrences across steps; also need subquery to find max test score per step/version for non-Stack models and compare with Stack model's test sco

records.question: Identify the L1_model associated with each model (specified by name and version) that occurs most frequently for each status ('strong' or 'soft'), along with the number of times it occurs. A model has a 'strong' status if, for any of its steps, the maximum test score among non-'Stack' models is less than the 'Stack' model's test score. It has a 'soft' status if the maximum test score among non-'Stack' models equals the 'Stack' model's test score. Count how many times each L1_model is associated with a 'strong' or 'soft' status across all models, and determine which L1_model has the highest occurrence for each status.
records.pred_sql:
WITH model_status AS (
    SELECT 
        m.name,
        m.version,
        m.L1_model,
        CASE 
            WHEN EXISTS (
                SELECT 1
                FROM model_score ms
                WHERE ms.name = m.name AND ms.version = m.version
                GROUP BY ms.step
                HAVING MAX(CASE WHEN ms.model != 'Stack' THEN ms.test_score END) < MAX(CASE WHEN ms.model = 'Stack' THEN ms.test_score END)
            ) THEN 'strong'
            WHEN EXISTS (
                SELECT 1
                FROM model_score ms
                WHERE ms.name = m.name AND ms.version = m.version
                GROUP BY ms.step
                HAVING MAX(CASE WHEN ms.model != 'Stack' THEN ms.test_score END) = MAX(CASE WHEN ms.model = 'Stack' THEN ms.test_score END)
            ) THEN 'soft'
        END AS status
    FROM model m
    WHERE EXISTS (
        SELECT 1 FROM model_score ms 
        WHERE ms.name = m.name AND ms.version = m.version AND ms.model = 'Stack'
    )
),
status_counts AS (
    SELECT 
        L1_model,
        status,
        COUNT(*) AS occurrence_count
    FROM model_status
    WHERE status IS NOT NULL
    GROUP BY L1_model, status
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT 
        L1_model,
        status,
        occurrence_count,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY status ORDER BY occurrence_count DESC) AS rn
    FROM status_counts
)
SELECT 
    L1_model,
    status,
    occurrence_count
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY status

query_skeleton.notes: Need subquery to compute strong/soft status per model, then group by status and L1_model, count occurrences, order by count descending, and limit to top per status.

records.question: What is the average total quantity across all final packaging combinations, considering only the leaf-level items within each combination after fully expanding any nested packaging relationships?
records.pred_sql:
WITH RECURSIVE expand(packaging_id, contains_id, qty, depth) AS (
    -- Base: direct leaf items (packaging that is not a parent)
    SELECT 
        pr.packaging_id,
        pr.contains_id,
        pr.qty,
        1
    FROM packaging_relations pr
    WHERE pr.contains_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT packaging_id FROM packaging_relations)
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive: expand nested packaging
    SELECT 
        e.packaging_id,
        pr.contains_id,
        e.qty * pr.qty,
        e.depth + 1
    FROM expand e
    JOIN packaging_relations pr ON e.contains_id = pr.packaging_id
)
SELECT 
    ROUND(AVG(total_qty), 4) AS avg_total_quantity
FROM (
    SELECT 
        e.packaging_id,
        SUM(e.qty) AS total_qty
    FROM expand e
    WHERE e.contains_id NOT IN (SELECT DISTINCT packaging_id FROM packaging_relations)
    GROUP BY e.packaging_id
) AS final_combinations

query_skeleton.notes: Need recursive CTE to expand nested packaging, then group by final packaging to compute average total quantity of leaf-level items

records.question: Which top-level packaging containers, meaning those not contained within any other packaging, have any item for which the total quantity accumulated across all nested levels in the hierarchy exceeds 500, and what are the names of both these containers and the corresponding items?
records.pred_sql:
-- Find top-level containers (not contained in any other packaging) and items where total nested quantity > 500
SELECT 
    p1.name AS container_name,
    p2.name AS item_name
FROM packaging p1
JOIN packaging_relations pr1 ON p1.id = pr1.packaging_id
JOIN packaging p2 ON pr1.contains_id = p2.id
WHERE p1.id NOT IN (
    SELECT DISTINCT pr2.contains_id
    FROM packaging_relations pr2
)
AND p1.id IN (
    SELECT pr3.packaging_id
    FROM packaging_relations pr3
    GROUP BY pr3.packaging_id, pr3.contains_id
    HAVING SUM(pr3.qty) > 500
)
GROUP BY p1.id, p2.id
HAVING SUM(pr1.qty) > 500

query_skeleton.notes: Need to find top-level containers (not contained in any other packaging) and items where total nested quantity > 500, requiring subquery for top-level filter, GROUP BY and HAVING for sum aggregation,

records.question: Calculate the average pick percentage for each product name, using a first-in-first-out approach that selects from inventory locations based on the earliest purchase date and smallest available quantity, ensuring that the picked quantity reflects only the overlapping range between each order’s required quantity and the inventory’s available quantity, and then grouping and ordering the results by product name?
records.pred_sql:
-- Compute average pick percentage per product name using FIFO allocation
WITH fifo_inventory AS (
    -- Rank inventory by earliest purchase_id (proxy for purchase date) and smallest qty
    SELECT 
        i.id,
        i.product_id,
        i.qty,
        i.purchase_id,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
            PARTITION BY i.product_id 
            ORDER BY i.purchase_id ASC, i.qty ASC
        ) AS rn
    FROM inventory i
),
order_requirements AS (
    -- Total required qty per order per product
    SELECT 
        ol.order_id,
        ol.product_id,
        SUM(ol.qty) AS required_qty
    FROM orderlines ol
    GROUP BY ol.order_id, ol.product_id
),
allocated_picks AS (
    -- FIFO allocation: assign inventory to orders, compute overlapping picked qty
    SELECT 
        pl.order_id,
        pl.product_id,
        pl.qty AS picked_qty,
        fi.qty AS available_qty,
        -- Overlapping range: min(picked_qty, available_qty) but only up to required
        MIN(pl.qty, fi.qty, or2.required_qty) AS allocated_qty
    FROM picking_line pl
    JOIN order_requirements or2 ON pl.order_id = or2.order_id AND pl.product_id = or2.product_id
    JOIN fifo_inventory fi ON pl.product_id = fi.product_id
    WHERE fi.rn = 1  -- Only the earliest/smallest inventory per product
),
product_pick_pct AS (
    -- Compute pick percentage per product per order (allocated / required)
    SELECT 
        p.name,
        (CAST(SUM(ap.allocated_qty) AS REAL) / NULLIF(SUM(or2.required_qty), 0)) * 100 AS pick_pct
    FROM allocated_picks ap
    JOIN order_requirements or2 ON ap.order_id = or2.order_id AND ap.product_id = or2.product_id
    JOIN products p ON ap.product_id = p.id
    GROUP BY p.name
)
-- Average pick percentage per product name, ordered by name
SELECT 
    name,
    AVG(pick_pct) AS avg_pick_percentage
FROM product_pick_pct
GROUP BY name
ORDER BY name

query_skeleton.notes: Requires subquery for FIFO allocation per product, then average pick percentage grouped by product name, ordered by name.

records.question: Based on monthly sales data starting in January 2016 and using a centered moving average to adjust for seasonality, which products had a seasonality-adjusted sales ratio that stayed consistently above 2 for every month in the year 2017?
records.pred_sql:
-- Compute seasonality-adjusted sales ratio using centered moving average
-- For each product and month in 2017, ratio = qty / centered_ma
-- Filter products where every month in 2017 has ratio > 2
WITH monthly_ma AS (
    -- Compute centered moving average (12-month centered) for each product
    SELECT 
        ms.product_id,
        ms.mth,
        ms.qty,
        AVG(ms.qty) OVER (
            PARTITION BY ms.product_id 
            ORDER BY ms.mth 
            ROWS BETWEEN 5 PRECEDING AND 6 FOLLOWING
        ) AS centered_ma
    FROM monthly_sales ms
    WHERE ms.mth >= '2016-01-01'  -- Starting from January 2016
),
ratio_2017 AS (
    -- Compute ratio for each month in 2017
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        mth,
        CAST(qty AS REAL) / NULLIF(centered_ma, 0) AS ratio
    FROM monthly_ma
    WHERE mth >= '2017-01-01' AND mth < '2018-01-01'
),
product_check AS (
    -- Check if every month in 2017 has ratio > 2
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        MIN(ratio) AS min_ratio,
        COUNT(*) AS month_count
    FROM ratio_2017
    GROUP BY product_id
    HAVING MIN(ratio) > 2 AND COUNT(*) = 12
)
-- Return product names
SELECT p.name
FROM products p
JOIN product_check pc ON p.id = pc.product_id
ORDER BY p.name

query_skeleton.notes: Need subquery for centered moving average calculation, group by product with HAVING to filter products where every month in 2017 has ratio > 2

records.question: What is the average forecasted annual sales for products 4160 and 7790 during 2018, using monthly sales data starting from January 2016 for the first 36 months, applying seasonality adjustments from time steps 7 through 30, and employing a weighted regression method to estimate sales?
records.pred_sql:
-- Step 1: Filter monthly sales for products 4160 and 7790, from Jan 2016 for 36 months (Jan 2016 to Dec 2018)
-- Step 2: For each product, compute seasonality factors using months 7-30 (Jul 2016 to Jun 2018) as the average monthly sales per product
-- Step 3: Adjust monthly sales by dividing by seasonality factor
-- Step 4: Use weighted regression (linear regression with weights = time step index) on adjusted sales to forecast 2018 monthly sales
-- Step 5: Sum forecasted monthly sales for 2018 to get annual forecast per product
-- Step 6: Average the two annual forecasts

WITH 
-- Base data: monthly sales for products 4160 and 7790, first 36 months from Jan 2016
base AS (
    SELECT 
        ms.product_id,
        ms.mth,
        ms.qty,
        -- Time step index starting at 1 for Jan 2016
        (julianday(ms.mth) - julianday('2016-01-01')) / 30 + 1 AS time_step
    FROM monthly_sales ms
    WHERE ms.product_id IN (4160, 7790)
      AND ms.mth >= '2016-01-01'
      AND ms.mth < '2019-01-01'  -- 36 months: Jan 2016 to Dec 2018
),
-- Seasonality factors: average monthly sales for time steps 7-30 per product
seasonality AS (
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        AVG(CAST(qty AS REAL)) AS seasonal_avg
    FROM base
    WHERE time_step BETWEEN 7 AND 30
    GROUP BY product_id
),
-- Adjusted sales: divide each month's qty by its product's seasonal average
adjusted AS (
    SELECT 
        b.product_id,
        b.mth,
        b.time_step,
        b.qty,
        s.seasonal_avg,
        CAST(b.qty AS REAL) / s.seasonal_avg AS adjusted_qty
    FROM base b
    JOIN seasonality s ON b.product_id = s.product_id
),
-- Weighted regression: for each product, compute slope and intercept using time_step as x, adjusted_qty as y, weight = time_step
-- Formulas: slope = (n*sum(w*x*y) - sum(w*x)*sum(w*y)) / (n*sum(w*x^2) - (sum(w*x))^2)
-- intercept = (sum(w*y) - slope*sum(w*x)) / n
-- where n = number of data points, w = time_step (weight)
regression_params AS (
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        COUNT(*) AS n,
        SUM(time_step * time_step * adjusted_qty) AS sum_wx2y,
        SUM(time_step * time_step) AS sum_wx2,
        SUM(time_step * adjusted_qty) AS sum_wxy,
        SUM(time_step) AS sum_wx,
        SUM(adjusted_qty) AS sum_wy
    FROM adjusted
    GROUP BY product_id
),
-- Compute slope and intercept per product
regression AS (
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        (n * sum_wxy - sum_wx * sum_wy) / (n * sum_wx2 - sum_wx * sum_wx) AS slope,
        (sum_wy - (n * sum_wxy - sum_wx * sum_wy) / (n * sum_wx2 - sum_wx * sum_wx) * sum_wx) / n AS intercept
    FROM regression_params
),
-- Forecast 2018 monthly sales (time steps 25-36) using regression: forecast = (slope * time_step + intercept) * seasonal_avg
forecast_2018 AS (
    SELECT 
        r.product_id,
        b.time_step,
        (r.slope * b.time_step + r.intercept) * b.seasonal_avg AS forecasted_qty
    FROM regression r
    JOIN adjusted b ON r.product_id = b.product_id
    WHERE b.time_step BETWEEN 25 AND 36  -- 2018 months: Jan 2018 = time_step 25, Dec 2018 = time_step 36
    GROUP BY r.product_id, b.time_step
),
-- Sum forecasted quantities for 2018 per product
annual_forecast AS (
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        SUM(forecasted_qty) AS annual_sales
    FROM forecast_2018
    GROUP BY product_id
)
-- Average of the two annual forecasts
SELECT AVG(annual_sales) AS avg_forecasted_annual_sales
FROM annual_forecast

query_skeleton.notes: Question asks for average forecasted annual sales, requiring aggregation (AVG) and likely a subquery for time-series calculations based on external knowledge.

records.question: Using a recursive monthly inventory adjustment model starting from December 2018 inventory levels, where we restock a product if its ending inventory drops below the minimum required level, determine for each product the month in 2019 where the absolute difference between its ending inventory and the minimum required level is the smallest, and return the product_id, that month, and the absolute difference.
records.pred_sql:
WITH RECURSIVE monthly_inventory AS (
    -- Base case: December 2018 inventory levels (assuming inventory records represent ending inventory)
    SELECT 
        i.product_id,
        i.qty AS ending_inventory,
        '2018-12' AS month
    FROM inventory i
    WHERE i.purchase_id IN (SELECT id FROM purchases WHERE strftime('%Y-%m', purchase_date) = '2018-12')
    
    UNION ALL
    
    -- Recursive step: for each subsequent month in 2019
    SELECT 
        mi.product_id,
        CASE 
            WHEN mi.ending_inventory < pm.qty_minimum THEN mi.ending_inventory + pm.qty_purchase - COALESCE(monthly_usage.qty_used, 0)
            ELSE mi.ending_inventory - COALESCE(monthly_usage.qty_used, 0)
        END AS ending_inventory,
        CASE 
            WHEN mi.month = '2018-12' THEN '2019-01'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-01' THEN '2019-02'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-02' THEN '2019-03'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-03' THEN '2019-04'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-04' THEN '2019-05'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-05' THEN '2019-06'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-06' THEN '2019-07'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-07' THEN '2019-08'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-08' THEN '2019-09'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-09' THEN '2019-10'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-10' THEN '2019-11'
            WHEN mi.month = '2019-11' THEN '2019-12'
        END AS month
    FROM monthly_inventory mi
    JOIN product_minimums pm ON mi.product_id = pm.product_id
    LEFT JOIN (
        SELECT 
            product_id,
            strftime('%Y-%m', purchase_date) AS month,
            SUM(qty) AS qty_used
        FROM inventory i
        JOIN purchases p ON i.purchase_id = p.id
        WHERE strftime('%Y', purchase_date) = '2019'
        GROUP BY product_id, month
    ) monthly_usage ON mi.product_id = monthly_usage.product_id AND mi.month = monthly_usage.month
    WHERE mi.month < '2019-12'
),
product_month_diff AS (
    SELECT 
        product_id,
        month,
        ABS(ending_inventory - pm.qty_minimum) AS abs_diff
    FROM monthly_inventory mi
    JOIN product_minimums pm ON mi.product_id = pm.product_id
    WHERE month LIKE '2019%'
)
SELECT 
    product_id,
    month,
    MIN(abs_diff) AS min_abs_diff
FROM product_month_diff
GROUP BY product_id
ORDER BY product_id

query_skeleton.notes: Need subquery for recursive monthly model; group by product to find min absolute difference per product; select product_id, month, difference

records.question: Analyze the soccer match dataset to determine the champion team for each season across all countries and leagues, awarding 3 points for every win, 1 point for every tie, and 0 points for every loss. For each season, return the champion’s team name, the league, the country, and the total points accumulated.
records.pred_sql:
-- Compute points per team per season per league per country, then select the champion (max points) per season
WITH team_points AS (
    SELECT
        m.season,
        t.team_long_name AS team_name,
        l.name AS league,
        c.name AS country,
        SUM(
            CASE
                WHEN (m.home_team_api_id = t.team_api_id AND m.home_team_goal > m.away_team_goal)
                     OR (m.away_team_api_id = t.team_api_id AND m.away_team_goal > m.home_team_goal)
                THEN 3
                WHEN (m.home_team_api_id = t.team_api_id AND m.home_team_goal = m.away_team_goal)
                     OR (m.away_team_api_id = t.team_api_id AND m.away_team_goal = m.home_team_goal)
                THEN 1
                ELSE 0
            END
        ) AS total_points
    FROM Match m
    JOIN Team t ON t.team_api_id IN (m.home_team_api_id, m.away_team_api_id)
    JOIN League l ON m.league_id = l.id
    JOIN Country c ON l.country_id = c.id
    GROUP BY m.season, t.team_api_id, l.id, c.id
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT
        season,
        team_name,
        league,
        country,
        total_points,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY season ORDER BY total_points DESC) AS rn
    FROM team_points
)
SELECT season, team_name, league, country, total_points
FROM ranked
WHERE rn = 1
ORDER BY season

query_skeleton.notes: Need to compute points per team per season using CASE for win/tie/loss, then group by season, team, league, country to find champion per season (max points).

records.question: For veg whsle data, can you generate a summary of our items' loss rates? Include the average loss rate, and also break down the count of items that are below, above, and within one standard deviation from this average.
records.pred_sql:
-- Compute average and standard deviation of loss rates, then count items in each category
WITH stats AS (
  SELECT 
    AVG(loss_rate_%) AS avg_loss,
    AVG(loss_rate_% * loss_rate_%) - AVG(loss_rate_%) * AVG(loss_rate_%) AS var_loss,
    SQRT(AVG(loss_rate_% * loss_rate_%) - AVG(loss_rate_%) * AVG(loss_rate_%)) AS std_loss
  FROM veg_loss_rate_df
)
SELECT 
  ROUND((SELECT avg_loss FROM stats), 4) AS average_loss_rate,
  SUM(CASE WHEN loss_rate_% < (SELECT avg_loss - std_loss FROM stats) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS below_one_std,
  SUM(CASE WHEN loss_rate_% > (SELECT avg_loss + std_loss FROM stats) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS above_one_std,
  SUM(CASE WHEN loss_rate_% BETWEEN (SELECT avg_loss - std_loss FROM stats) AND (SELECT avg_loss + std_loss FROM stats) THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS within_one_std
FROM veg_loss_rate_df

query_skeleton.notes: Question asks for average loss rate and counts of items below, above, and within one standard deviation from average. This requires computing AVG and STDDEV (aggregation) and then comparing each item'

records.question: For veg whsle data, can you analyze our financial performance over the years 2020 to 2023? I need insights into the average wholesale price, maximum wholesale price, minimum wholesale price, wholesale price difference, total wholesale price, total selling price, average loss rate, total loss, and profit for each category within each year. Round all calculated values to two decimal places.
records.pred_sql:
SELECT 
    c.category_name,
    CAST(strftime('%Y', w.whsle_date) AS INTEGER) AS year,
    ROUND(AVG(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg), 2) AS avg_wholesale_price,
    ROUND(MAX(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg), 2) AS max_wholesale_price,
    ROUND(MIN(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg), 2) AS min_wholesale_price,
    ROUND(MAX(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg) - MIN(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg), 2) AS wholesale_price_difference,
    ROUND(SUM(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg), 2) AS total_wholesale_price,
    ROUND(SUM(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg / (1.0 - l.loss_rate_pct / 100.0)), 2) AS total_selling_price,
    ROUND(AVG(l.loss_rate_pct), 2) AS avg_loss_rate,
    ROUND(SUM(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg * (l.loss_rate_pct / 100.0)), 2) AS total_loss,
    ROUND(SUM(w.whsle_px_rmb_kg / (1.0 - l.loss_rate_pct / 100.0) - w.whsle_px_rmb_kg), 2) AS profit
FROM veg_whsle_df w
JOIN veg_cat c ON w.item_code = c.item_code
JOIN veg_loss_rate_df l ON w.item_code = l.item_code
WHERE CAST(strftime('%Y', w.whsle_date) AS INTEGER) BETWEEN 2020 AND 2023
GROUP BY c.category_name, year
ORDER BY c.category_name, year

query_skeleton.notes: Question asks for per-category-per-year aggregated metrics (avg, max, min, diff, sum, loss rate, profit) requiring GROUP BY on year and category.

records.question: Prepare a comprehensive performance report on our sellers, focusing on total sales, average item price, average review scores, and packing times. Ensure that the report includes only those sellers who have sold a quantity of more than 100 products and highlight the product category names in English with the highest sales volume.
records.pred_sql:
-- Step 1: Compute per-seller metrics: total sales (sum of price), average item price, quantity sold, and packing time (days between order_purchase_timestamp and order_delivered_carrier_date)
-- Step 2: Join with product_category_name_translation to get English category names
-- Step 3: Identify the category with highest sales volume per seller (subquery)
-- Step 4: Filter sellers with quantity > 100
-- Step 5: Order by total sales descending, limit to top sellers

WITH seller_metrics AS (
    SELECT 
        s.seller_id,
        SUM(oi.price) AS total_sales,
        AVG(oi.price) AS avg_item_price,
        COUNT(oi.order_id) AS quantity_sold,
        AVG(
            CAST(JULIANDAY(o.order_delivered_carrier_date) - JULIANDAY(o.order_purchase_timestamp) AS REAL)
        ) AS avg_packing_time,
        -- For each seller, find the product category with highest sales volume
        -- We'll use a subquery later to get the top category
        oi.product_id
    FROM sellers s
    JOIN order_items oi ON s.seller_id = oi.seller_id
    JOIN orders o ON oi.order_id = o.order_id
    GROUP BY s.seller_id
    HAVING quantity_sold > 100
),
-- Get product category for each order item
product_categories AS (
    SELECT 
        oi.seller_id,
        pct.product_category_name_english,
        SUM(oi.price) AS category_sales
    FROM order_items oi
    JOIN products p ON oi.product_id = p.product_id
    JOIN product_category_name_translation pct ON p.product_category_name = pct.product_category_name
    GROUP BY oi.seller_id, pct.product_category_name_english
),
-- For each seller, get the category with highest sales
top_categories AS (
    SELECT 
        seller_id,
        product_category_name_english AS top_category,
        category_sales,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY seller_id ORDER BY category_sales DESC) AS rn
    FROM product_categories
)
SELECT 
    sm.seller_id,
    sm.total_sales,
    sm.avg_item_price,
    -- Note: review_scores not available in schema, so we'll omit or use NULL
    NULL AS avg_review_score,
    sm.avg_packing_time,
    tc.top_category
FROM seller_metrics sm
LEFT JOIN top_categories tc ON sm.seller_id = tc.seller_id AND tc.rn = 1
ORDER BY sm.total_sales DESC
LIMIT 100

query_skeleton.notes: Requires grouping by seller_id, having sum(quantity) > 100, subquery for top category by sales, multiple aggregations, and ordering by sales volume.

records.question: For weekly-sales data, I need an analysis of our sales performance around mid-June for the years 2018, 2019, and 2020. Specifically, calculate the percentage change in sales between the four weeks leading up to June 15 and the four weeks following June 15 for each year.
records.pred_sql:
WITH sales_before AS (
    SELECT 
        calendar_year,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales_before
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE 
        calendar_year IN (2018, 2019, 2020)
        AND week_date_formatted >= CAST(calendar_year AS TEXT) || '-5-18'
        AND week_date_formatted < CAST(calendar_year AS TEXT) || '-6-15'
    GROUP BY calendar_year
),
sales_after AS (
    SELECT 
        calendar_year,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales_after
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE 
        calendar_year IN (2018, 2019, 2020)
        AND week_date_formatted >= CAST(calendar_year AS TEXT) || '-6-15'
        AND week_date_formatted < CAST(calendar_year AS TEXT) || '-7-13'
    GROUP BY calendar_year
)
SELECT 
    b.calendar_year,
    ROUND(
        (CAST(a.total_sales_after AS REAL) - b.total_sales_before) / b.total_sales_before * 100, 
        4
    ) AS percentage_change
FROM sales_before b
JOIN sales_after a ON b.calendar_year = a.calendar_year
ORDER BY b.calendar_year

query_skeleton.notes: Need to compute percentage change between two aggregated sales sums (4 weeks before and after June 15) for each year, requiring GROUP BY year and aggregation of sales.

records.question: Analyze the average percentage change in sales between the 12 weeks before and after June 15, 2020, for each attribute type: region, platform, age band, demographic, and customer type. For each attribute type, calculate the average percentage change in sales across all its attribute values. Identify the attribute type with the highest negative impact on sales and provide its average percentage change in sales.
records.pred_sql:
WITH 
-- Define the reference date
ref_date AS (
    SELECT '2020-06-15' AS ref_date
),
-- Get the week number of the reference date
ref_week AS (
    SELECT week_number 
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales 
    WHERE week_date = (SELECT ref_date FROM ref_date)
    LIMIT 1
),
-- Compute sales per attribute value for 12 weeks before June 15, 2020
before_sales AS (
    SELECT 
        'region' AS attr_type,
        region AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number < (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number >= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) - 12
    GROUP BY region
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 
        'platform' AS attr_type,
        platform AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number < (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number >= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) - 12
    GROUP BY platform
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 
        'age_band' AS attr_type,
        age_band AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number < (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number >= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) - 12
    GROUP BY age_band
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 
        'demographic' AS attr_type,
        demographic AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number < (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number >= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) - 12
    GROUP BY demographic
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 
        'customer_type' AS attr_type,
        customer_type AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number < (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number >= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) - 12
    GROUP BY customer_type
),
-- Compute sales per attribute value for 12 weeks after June 15, 2020
after_sales AS (
    SELECT 
        'region' AS attr_type,
        region AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number > (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number <= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) + 12
    GROUP BY region
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 
        'platform' AS attr_type,
        platform AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number > (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number <= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) + 12
    GROUP BY platform
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 
        'age_band' AS attr_type,
        age_band AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number > (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number <= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) + 12
    GROUP BY age_band
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 
        'demographic' AS attr_type,
        demographic AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number > (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number <= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) + 12
    GROUP BY demographic
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 
        'customer_type' AS attr_type,
        customer_type AS attr_value,
        SUM(sales) AS total_sales
    FROM cleaned_weekly_sales
    WHERE week_number > (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week)
      AND week_number <= (SELECT week_number FROM ref_week) + 12
    GROUP BY customer_type
),
-- Compute percentage change per attribute value
pct_change AS (
    SELECT 
        b.attr_type,
        b.attr_value,
        CASE 
            WHEN b.total_sales = 0 THEN NULL
            ELSE (CAST(a.total_sales AS REAL) - CAST(b.total_sales AS REAL)) / CAST(b.total_sales AS REAL) * 100.0
        END AS pct_change
    FROM before_sales b
    JOIN after_sales a ON b.attr_type = a.attr_type AND b.attr_value = a.attr_value
),
-- Average percentage change per attribute type
avg_pct_change AS (
    SELECT 
        attr_type,
        AVG(pct_change) AS avg_pct_change
    FROM pct_change
    GROUP BY attr_type
)
-- Select the attribute type with the highest negative impact (lowest avg_pct_change)
SELECT 
    attr_type,
    ROUND(avg_pct_change, 4) AS avg_pct_change
FROM avg_pct_change
ORDER BY avg_pct_change ASC
LIMIT 1

query_skeleton.notes: Need subquery to compute before/after sales per attribute value, then group by attribute type to average percentage changes, order by avg change ascending, limit 1 for highest negative impact.

records.question: Using the activity log table, compute the total number of unique user sessions where each web page appears as either a landing page (the first page visited in a session based on timestamp) or an exit page (the last page visited in a session based on timestamp), or both. Count each session only once per page even if the page serves as both landing and exit for that session.
records.pred_sql:
-- Step 1: Find the first page (landing) per session based on earliest timestamp
WITH landing_pages AS (
    SELECT 
        session,
        path AS page
    FROM activity_log
    WHERE (session, stamp) IN (
        SELECT session, MIN(stamp)
        FROM activity_log
        GROUP BY session
    )
),
-- Step 2: Find the last page (exit) per session based on latest timestamp
exit_pages AS (
    SELECT 
        session,
        path AS page
    FROM activity_log
    WHERE (session, stamp) IN (
        SELECT session, MAX(stamp)
        FROM activity_log
        GROUP BY session
    )
),
-- Step 3: Combine landing and exit pages, deduplicating per session and page
combined AS (
    SELECT session, page FROM landing_pages
    UNION
    SELECT session, page FROM exit_pages
)
-- Step 4: Count distinct sessions per page
SELECT 
    page,
    COUNT(DISTINCT session) AS total_unique_sessions
FROM combined
GROUP BY page
ORDER BY page

query_skeleton.notes: Need subqueries to find first and last page per session, then union them, then group by page to count distinct sessions.

records.question: Which three distinct third-page visits are most frequently observed immediately after two consecutive visits to the '/detail' page, and how many times does each third-page visit occur?
records.pred_sql:
-- Step 1: Find sessions with two consecutive '/detail' visits (ordered by stamp)
-- Step 2: For each such pair, get the next page (third-page visit) in the same session
-- Step 3: Count occurrences of each third-page visit, order descending, limit 3
WITH ordered_logs AS (
    SELECT 
        session,
        action,
        stamp,
        LAG(action, 1) OVER (PARTITION BY session ORDER BY stamp) AS prev_action1,
        LAG(action, 2) OVER (PARTITION BY session ORDER BY stamp) AS prev_action2
    FROM access_log
),
consecutive_detail_pairs AS (
    SELECT 
        session,
        stamp AS pair_end_stamp,
        action AS third_action
    FROM ordered_logs
    WHERE prev_action1 = '/detail' AND prev_action2 = '/detail'
      AND action != '/detail'  -- third page is distinct from '/detail'
),
third_page_counts AS (
    SELECT 
        third_action,
        COUNT(*) AS visit_count
    FROM consecutive_detail_pairs
    GROUP BY third_action
)
SELECT 
    third_action,
    visit_count
FROM third_page_counts
ORDER BY visit_count DESC
LIMIT 3

query_skeleton.notes: Need subquery to find sessions with two consecutive '/detail' visits, then count third-page visits per distinct page, order by count descending, limit 3

records.question: For each user session in the activity log table, identify the number of events that occurred before the first '/detail' click or '/complete' conversion, counting only events that have a non-empty search type. Find the sessions with the minimum count of such pre-click/pre-conversion events. If multiple sessions share this minimum count, include all of them in the results. Return each qualifying session along with the corresponding path and search type.

WITH session_first_event AS (
    SELECT 
        session,
        MIN(stamp) AS first_event_stamp
    FROM activity_log
    WHERE path IN ('/detail', '/complete')
    GROUP BY session
),
pre_events AS (
    SELECT 
        a.session,
        a.path,
        a.search_type,
        COUNT(*) AS event_count
    FROM activity_log a
    JOIN session_first_event sfe ON a.session = sfe.session
    WHERE a.stamp < sfe.first_event_stamp
      AND a.search_type IS NOT NULL
      AND a.search_type != ''
    GROUP BY a.session, a.path, a.search_type
),
min_count AS (
    SELECT MIN(event_count) AS min_event_count
    FROM pre_events
)
SELECT 
    p.session,
    p.path,
    p.search_type
FROM pre_events p
JOIN min_count m ON p.event_count = m.min_event_count
ORDER BY p.session

records.question: Considering all races where pit stop data is available, and focusing on instances when a driver was not behind another car on the previous lap but is behind on the current lap (accounting for retirements, pit-stop entries, pit-stop exits, and race starts), how many times has each type of overtake occurred in Formula 1?

records.question: In Formula 1 seasons since 2001, considering only drivers who scored points in a season, which five constructors have had the most seasons where their drivers scored the fewest total points among all point-scoring drivers in that season?
records.pred_sql:
WITH driver_season_points AS (
    -- Total points per driver per season (only point-scoring drivers)
    SELECT 
        r.year,
        res.driver_id,
        SUM(res.points) AS total_points
    FROM results res
    JOIN races r ON res.race_id = r.race_id
    WHERE r.year >= 2001
    GROUP BY r.year, res.driver_id
    HAVING SUM(res.points) > 0
),
season_min_points AS (
    -- Minimum total points among point-scoring drivers per season
    SELECT 
        year,
        MIN(total_points) AS min_points
    FROM driver_season_points
    GROUP BY year
),
season_min_drivers AS (
    -- Drivers who achieved the minimum points in each season
    SELECT 
        dsp.year,
        dsp.driver_id
    FROM driver_season_points dsp
    JOIN season_min_points smp ON dsp.year = smp.year AND dsp.total_points = smp.min_points
),
constructor_seasons AS (
    -- For each constructor, count seasons where they had a driver with minimum points
    SELECT 
        c.constructor_id,
        c.name,
        COUNT(DISTINCT smd.year) AS seasons_count
    FROM season_min_drivers smd
    JOIN results res ON smd.driver_id = res.driver_id AND smd.year = (SELECT r.year FROM races r WHERE r.race_id = res.race_id)
    JOIN constructors c ON res.constructor_id = c.constructor_id
    GROUP BY c.constructor_id, c.name
)
SELECT 
    name
FROM constructor_seasons
ORDER BY seasons_count DESC, name ASC
LIMIT 5

records.question: For each year, which driver and which constructor scored the most points? I want the full name of each driver.
records.pred_sql:
-- For each year, find the driver and constructor with the most points
-- Driver: use driver_standings.points per race, sum per year, then get max per year
-- Constructor: use constructor_results.points per race, sum per year, then get max per year
-- Combine results per year

WITH driver_points_per_year AS (
    SELECT 
        r.year,
        d.driver_id,
        d.forename || ' ' || d.surname AS driver_full_name,
        SUM(ds.points) AS total_points
    FROM driver_standings ds
    JOIN races r ON ds.race_id = r.race_id
    JOIN drivers d ON ds.driver_id = d.driver_id
    GROUP BY r.year, d.driver_id, d.forename, d.surname
),
top_driver_per_year AS (
    SELECT 
        year,
        driver_full_name,
        total_points
    FROM driver_points_per_year
    WHERE (year, total_points) IN (
        SELECT year, MAX(total_points)
        FROM driver_points_per_year
        GROUP BY year
    )
),
constructor_points_per_year AS (
    SELECT 
        r.year,
        c.constructor_id,
        c.name AS constructor_name,
        SUM(cr.points) AS total_points
    FROM constructor_results cr
    JOIN races r ON cr.race_id = r.race_id
    JOIN constructors c ON cr.constructor_id = c.constructor_id
    GROUP BY r.year, c.constructor_id, c.name
),
top_constructor_per_year AS (
    SELECT 
        year,
        constructor_name,
        total_points
    FROM constructor_points_per_year
    WHERE (year, total_points) IN (
        SELECT year, MAX(total_points)
        FROM constructor_points_per_year
        GROUP BY year
    )
)
SELECT 
    tdy.year,
    tdy.driver_full_name,
    tcy.constructor_name
FROM top_driver_per_year tdy
JOIN top_constructor_per_year tcy ON tdy.year = tcy.year
ORDER BY tdy.year

query_skeleton.notes: For each year, need to find driver and constructor with max points, requiring subqueries per year for both entities.

records.question: Using only the data from the ‘results’ table, find the three years in which the sum of the highest total points earned by any driver and the highest total points earned by any constructor in that year (both calculated by summing up points from the ‘results’ table) is smallest, and list those three years in order of ascending total.
records.pred_sql:
SELECT 
    (
        SELECT MAX(driver_points.total)
        FROM (
            SELECT results.driver_id, SUM(results.points) AS total
            FROM results
            JOIN races ON results.race_id = races.race_id
            WHERE races.year = r.year
            GROUP BY results.driver_id
        ) AS driver_points
    ) +
    (
        SELECT MAX(constructor_points.total)
        FROM (
            SELECT results.constructor_id, SUM(results.points) AS total
            FROM results
            JOIN races ON results.race_id = races.race_id
            WHERE races.year = r.year
            GROUP BY results.constructor_id
        ) AS constructor_points
    ) AS total_sum
FROM races AS r
GROUP BY r.year
ORDER BY total_sum ASC
LIMIT 3

query_skeleton.notes: Need to compute per-year sum of max driver points and max constructor points, then find three smallest sums, requiring subqueries for per-group maxima and outer aggregation.

records.question: Which constructors had the top 3 combined points from their best driver and team, and in which years did they achieve them?
records.pred_sql:
-- Step 1: For each constructor, year, and driver, compute the total points earned by that driver for that constructor in that year.
-- Step 2: For each constructor and year, find the maximum points among all drivers (the "best driver" points).
-- Step 3: Also for each constructor and year, compute the total points earned by the constructor (sum of all driver points) as "team points".
-- Step 4: Combine best driver points + team points per constructor per year.
-- Step 5: Rank these combined points per year and select top 3 per year.
-- Step 6: Output constructor name, year, and combined points.

WITH driver_points_per_year AS (
    SELECT 
        r.constructor_id,
        ra.year,
        r.driver_id,
        SUM(r.points) AS driver_total_points
    FROM results r
    JOIN races ra ON r.race_id = ra.race_id
    GROUP BY r.constructor_id, ra.year, r.driver_id
),
best_driver_per_constructor_year AS (
    SELECT 
        constructor_id,
        year,
        MAX(driver_total_points) AS best_driver_points
    FROM driver_points_per_year
    GROUP BY constructor_id, year
),
team_points_per_year AS (
    SELECT 
        r.constructor_id,
        ra.year,
        SUM(r.points) AS team_points
    FROM results r
    JOIN races ra ON r.race_id = ra.race_id
    GROUP BY r.constructor_id, ra.year
),
combined_points AS (
    SELECT 
        b.constructor_id,
        b.year,
        b.best_driver_points + t.team_points AS combined_points
    FROM best_driver_per_constructor_year b
    JOIN team_points_per_year t ON b.constructor_id = t.constructor_id AND b.year = t.year
),
ranked AS (
    SELECT 
        c.constructor_id,
        c.year,
        c.combined_points,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY c.year ORDER BY c.combined_points DESC) AS rn
    FROM combined_points c
)
SELECT 
    con.name AS constructor,
    r.year,
    r.combined_points
FROM ranked r
JOIN constructors con ON r.constructor_id = con.constructor_id
WHERE r.rn <= 3
ORDER BY r.year, r.combined_points DESC

query_skeleton.notes: Need to find top 3 constructors by combined points from best driver and team per year, requiring subquery to get per-driver max points per constructor per year, then group by constructor and year, ord

records.question: Among Formula 1 drivers who raced during the 1950s, which drivers completed a season in that decade with the same constructor in both the first and the last race they participated in, while also taking part in at least two distinct race rounds during that season?
records.pred_sql:
SELECT d.driver_id, d.forename, d.surname
FROM drivers d
JOIN results r ON d.driver_id = r.driver_id
JOIN races ra ON r.race_id = ra.race_id
JOIN constructors c ON r.constructor_id = c.constructor_id
WHERE ra.year >= 1950 AND ra.year <= 1959
GROUP BY d.driver_id, ra.year
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT ra.round) >= 2
   AND MIN(ra.round) = (SELECT MIN(r2.round) FROM results r2 JOIN races ra2 ON r2.race_id = ra2.race_id WHERE r2.driver_id = d.driver_id AND ra2.year = ra.year AND r2.constructor_id = c.constructor_id)
   AND MAX(ra.round) = (SELECT MAX(r3.round) FROM results r3 JOIN races ra3 ON r3.race_id = ra3.race_id WHERE r3.driver_id = d.driver_id AND ra3.year = ra.year AND r3.constructor_id = c.constructor_id)

query_skeleton.notes: Need to group by driver and season (year) to check first/last constructor equality and count distinct rounds >= 2

records.question: Calculate the overall average first round and average last round of races missed by Formula 1 drivers across all years. Include only drivers who missed fewer than three races in a given year and who switched teams between their appearances immediately before and after their hiatus (i.e., the constructor ID for the race right before their first missed race must be different from the constructor ID for the race right after their last missed race in that year). Do not group results by year; return just the overall averages across the entire dataset.
records.pred_sql:
-- Compute overall average first_round and last_round for drivers meeting the criteria
SELECT 
    AVG(missed.first_round) AS avg_first_round,
    AVG(missed.last_round) AS avg_last_round
FROM (
    -- For each driver and year, find the first and last missed round (consecutive missed races)
    SELECT 
        d.driver_id,
        r.year,
        MIN(r.round) AS first_round,
        MAX(r.round) AS last_round,
        COUNT(*) AS missed_count
    FROM results res
    JOIN races r ON res.race_id = r.race_id
    JOIN drivers d ON res.driver_id = d.driver_id
    WHERE res.position IS NULL  -- missed race (did not finish / not classified)
    GROUP BY d.driver_id, r.year
    HAVING missed_count < 3  -- fewer than three missed races in that year
) AS missed
-- Join to get constructor for race before first missed race
JOIN results res_before ON res_before.driver_id = missed.driver_id 
    AND res_before.race_id = (
        SELECT r2.race_id 
        FROM races r2 
        WHERE r2.year = missed.year 
          AND r2.round = missed.first_round - 1
    )
-- Join to get constructor for race after last missed race
JOIN results res_after ON res_after.driver_id = missed.driver_id 
    AND res_after.race_id = (
        SELECT r3.race_id 
        FROM races r3 
        WHERE r3.year = missed.year 
          AND r3.round = missed.last_round + 1
    )
WHERE res_before.constructor_id != res_after.constructor_id;  -- team switch condition

query_skeleton.notes: Need subqueries to identify missed races and check team switch condition, then compute overall averages of first_round and last_round across all years

records.question: Provide the full names of drivers who have been overtaken on track more times than they have overtaken others on track during race laps, excluding position changes due to pit stops (both at pit entry and exit), retirements, or position changes that occurred during the first lap of a race (considered as start movements).
records.pred_sql:
WITH overtaken AS (
    -- Count times a driver was overtaken (position increased, i.e., got worse)
    SELECT 
        lp1.driver_id,
        COUNT(*) AS overtaken_count
    FROM lap_positions lp1
    JOIN lap_positions lp2 
        ON lp1.race_id = lp2.race_id 
        AND lp1.lap = lp2.lap 
        AND lp1.driver_id != lp2.driver_id
    WHERE lp1.position > lp2.position  -- driver was overtaken (moved to higher position number)
        AND lp1.lap > 1  -- exclude first lap
        AND lp1.lap_type NOT IN ('PIT', 'PIT_EXIT', 'RETIRED')  -- exclude pit stops and retirements
    GROUP BY lp1.driver_id
),
overtaking AS (
    -- Count times a driver overtook others (position decreased, i.e., got better)
    SELECT 
        lp1.driver_id,
        COUNT(*) AS overtaking_count
    FROM lap_positions lp1
    JOIN lap_positions lp2 
        ON lp1.race_id = lp2.race_id 
        AND lp1.lap = lp2.lap 
        AND lp1.driver_id != lp2.driver_id
    WHERE lp1.position < lp2.position  -- driver overtook (moved to lower position number)
        AND lp1.lap > 1  -- exclude first lap
        AND lp1.lap_type NOT IN ('PIT', 'PIT_EXIT', 'RETIRED')  -- exclude pit stops and retirements
    GROUP BY lp1.driver_id
)
SELECT d.full_name
FROM drivers d
JOIN overtaken ot ON d.driver_id = ot.driver_id
JOIN overtaking o ON d.driver_id = o.driver_id
WHERE ot.overtaken_count > o.overtaking_count
ORDER BY d.full_name

query_skeleton.notes: Need to compute overtakes per driver using lap positions, exclude pit stops, retirements, and first lap; compare counts with HAVING; subquery for overtaken vs overtaking counts.

records.question: For each year from 2015 to 2020, which Census Zip Code Tabulation Area had the second-highest annual population growth rate? Include only areas with a population estimate of at least 25,000 people in that year (based on 5-Year American Community Survey estimates). For each year, provide the zip code, state abbreviation, and the annual growth rate percentage.
records.pred_sql:
WITH ranked AS (
  SELECT 
    EXTRACT(YEAR FROM t.DATE) AS year,
    t.GEO_ID,
    a.VARIABLE_NAME,
    t.VALUE::NUMBER AS pop_est,
    LAG(t.VALUE::NUMBER) OVER (PARTITION BY t.GEO_ID ORDER BY t.DATE) AS prev_pop,
    (t.VALUE::NUMBER - LAG(t.VALUE::NUMBER) OVER (PARTITION BY t.GEO_ID ORDER BY t.DATE)) / NULLIF(LAG(t.VALUE::NUMBER) OVER (PARTITION BY t.GEO_ID ORDER BY t.DATE), 0) * 100 AS growth_rate
  FROM GLOBAL_GOVERNMENT.CYBERSYN.AMERICAN_COMMUNITY_SURVEY_TIMESERIES t
  JOIN GLOBAL_GOVERNMENT.CYBERSYN.AMERICAN_COMMUNITY_SURVEY_ATTRIBUTES a
    ON t.VARIABLE = a.VARIABLE
  WHERE a.CENSUS_SUBJECT LIKE '%Industry, Occupation, and Class of Worke%'
    AND a.MEASUREMENT_TYPE = 'Estimate'
    AND a.MEASURE = 'Total'
    AND a.UNIT = 'People'
    AND EXTRACT(YEAR FROM t.DATE) BETWEEN 2015 AND 2020
    AND t.VALUE::NUMBER >= 25000
),
ranked2 AS (
  SELECT 
    year,
    GEO_ID,
    growth_rate,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY year ORDER BY growth_rate DESC) AS rn
  FROM ranked
  WHERE prev_pop IS NOT NULL
)
SELECT 
  r.year,
  SUBSTR(r.GEO_ID, 8) AS zip_code,
  g.GEO_ID AS state_abbreviation,
  ROUND(r.growth_rate, 2) AS annual_growth_rate_percentage
FROM ranked2 r
JOIN GLOBAL_GOVERNMENT.CYBERSYN.GEOGRAPHY_INDEX g
  ON r.GEO_ID = g.GEO_ID
WHERE r.rn = 2
ORDER BY r.year

records.question: What was the percentage change in post-market close prices for the Magnificent 7 tech companies from January 1 to June 30, 2024?
records.pred_sql:
WITH mag7_tickers AS (
  SELECT 'AAPL' AS ticker UNION ALL SELECT 'AMZN' UNION ALL SELECT 'GOOGL' UNION ALL SELECT 'META' UNION ALL SELECT 'MSFT' UNION ALL SELECT 'NVDA' UNION ALL SELECT 'TSLA'
),
post_market_close AS (
  SELECT ticker, date, value AS post_close_price
  FROM FINANCE__ECONOMICS.CYBERSYN.STOCK_PRICE_TIMESERIES
  WHERE variable = 'Post-Market Close'
    AND ticker IN (SELECT ticker FROM mag7_tickers)
    AND date IN ('2024-01-01', '2024-06-30')
)
SELECT 
  a.ticker,
  ((b.post_close_price - a.post_close_price) / a.post_close_price) * 100 AS pct_change
FROM post_market_close a
JOIN post_market_close b ON a.ticker = b.ticker AND a.date = '2024-01-01' AND b.date = '2024-06-30'
ORDER BY a.ticker

records.question: For each U.S. state, find how the number of active financial branch entities has changed from March 1, 2020, to December 31, 2021. An entity is considered active on a specific date if its start date is on or before that date and its end date is either null or on or after that date. For each state, calculate the number of entities active on March 1, 2020, the number of entities active on December 31, 2021, and the percentage change in these counts
records.pred_sql:
SELECT
    fbe.STATE_ABBREVIATION AS state,
    COUNT(CASE WHEN fbe.START_DATE <= '2020-03-01' AND (fbe.END_DATE IS NULL OR fbe.END_DATE >= '2020-03-01') THEN 1 END) AS active_2020_03_01,
    COUNT(CASE WHEN fbe.START_DATE <= '2021-12-31' AND (fbe.END_DATE IS NULL OR fbe.END_DATE >= '2021-12-31') THEN 1 END) AS active_2021_12_31,
    ROUND(
        (COUNT(CASE WHEN fbe.START_DATE <= '2021-12-31' AND (fbe.END_DATE IS NULL OR fbe.END_DATE >= '2021-12-31') THEN 1 END) - 
         COUNT(CASE WHEN fbe.START_DATE <= '2020-03-01' AND (fbe.END_DATE IS NULL OR fbe.END_DATE >= '2020-03-01') THEN 1 END)) * 100.0 / 
        NULLIF(COUNT(CASE WHEN fbe.START_DATE <= '2020-03-01' AND (fbe.END_DATE IS NULL OR fbe.END_DATE >= '2020-03-01') THEN 1 END), 0), 2
    ) AS percentage_change
FROM FINANCE__ECONOMICS.CYBERSYN.FINANCIAL_BRANCH_ENTITIES fbe
WHERE fbe.STATE_ABBREVIATION IS NOT NULL
GROUP BY fbe.STATE_ABBREVIATION
ORDER BY fbe.STATE_ABBREVIATION

records.question: How can we find the shortest straight-line distance in miles between each 'The Home Depot' store and its nearest 'Lowe's Home Improvement' location? Using the US_REAL_ESTATE.CYBERSYN database, join the point_of_interest_index table with point_of_interest_addresses_relationships and us_addresses tables to get geographic coordinates. For each 'The Home Depot' location (identified by its poi_id), calculate its distance to all 'Lowe's Home Improvement' stores using ST_DISTANCE and ST_MAKEPOINT functions, convert the distance from meters to miles (dividing by 1609), and return only the record with the minimum distance for each 'The Home Depot' store using QUALIFY with ROW_NUMBER().
records.pred_sql:
SELECT
    hd.poi_id AS home_depot_poi_id,
    hd.poi_name AS home_depot_name,
    MIN(ST_DISTANCE(ST_MAKEPOINT(hd_addr.longitude, hd_addr.latitude), ST_MAKEPOINT(lowes_addr.longitude, lowes_addr.latitude)) / 1609.344) AS distance_miles
FROM US_REAL_ESTATE.CYBERSYN.POINT_OF_INTEREST_INDEX hd
JOIN US_REAL_ESTATE.CYBERSYN.POINT_OF_INTEREST_ADDRESSES_RELATIONSHIPS hd_rel
    ON hd.poi_id = hd_rel.poi_id
JOIN US_REAL_ESTATE.CYBERSYN.US_ADDRESSES hd_addr
    ON hd_rel.address_id = hd_addr.address_id
CROSS JOIN US_REAL_ESTATE.CYBERSYN.POINT_OF_INTEREST_INDEX lowes
JOIN US_REAL_ESTATE.CYBERSYN.POINT_OF_INTEREST_ADDRESSES_RELATIONSHIPS lowes_rel
    ON lowes.poi_id = lowes_rel.poi_id
JOIN US_REAL_ESTATE.CYBERSYN.US_ADDRESSES lowes_addr
    ON lowes_rel.address_id = lowes_addr.address_id
WHERE hd.poi_name = 'The Home Depot'
  AND lowes.poi_name LIKE '%Lowe%'
GROUP BY hd.poi_id, hd.poi_name
QUALIFY ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY hd.poi_id ORDER BY distance_miles ASC) = 1

records.question: Examine user engagement with push notifications within a specified one-hour window on June 1, 2023.
records.pred_sql:
SELECT 
    s.APP_GROUP_ID,
    s.CAMPAIGN_ID,
    s.USER_ID,
    s.MESSAGE_VARIATION_ID,
    s.PLATFORM,
    s.AD_TRACKING_ENABLED,
    COUNT(DISTINCT s.ID) AS push_notification_sends,
    COUNT(DISTINCT s.USER_ID) AS unique_push_notification_sends,
    COUNT(DISTINCT b.ID) AS push_notification_bounced,
    COUNT(DISTINCT b.USER_ID) AS unique_push_notification_bounced,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.ID) AS push_notification_open,
    COUNT(DISTINCT o.USER_ID) AS unique_push_notification_opened,
    COUNT(DISTINCT io.ID) AS push_notification_influenced_open,
    COUNT(DISTINCT io.USER_ID) AS unique_push_notification_influenced_open
FROM BRAZE_USER_EVENT_DEMO_DATASET.PUBLIC.USERS_MESSAGES_PUSHNOTIFICATION_SEND_VIEW s
LEFT JOIN BRAZE_USER_EVENT_DEMO_DATASET.PUBLIC.USERS_MESSAGES_PUSHNOTIFICATION_BOUNCE_VIEW b
    ON s.ID = b.SEND_ID 
    AND s.USER_ID = b.USER_ID
    AND b.TIME BETWEEN 1717200000 AND 1717203600
LEFT JOIN BRAZE_USER_EVENT_DEMO_DATASET.PUBLIC.USERS_MESSAGES_PUSHNOTIFICATION_OPEN_VIEW o
    ON s.ID = o.SEND_ID 
    AND s.USER_ID = o.USER_ID
    AND o.TIME BETWEEN 1717200000 AND 1717203600
LEFT JOIN BRAZE_USER_EVENT_DEMO_DATASET.PUBLIC.USERS_MESSAGES_PUSHNOTIFICATION_INFLUENCEDOPEN_VIEW io
    ON s.ID = io.SEND_ID 
    AND s.USER_ID = io.USER_ID
    AND io.TIME BETWEEN 1717200000 AND 1717203600
WHERE s.TIME BETWEEN 1717200000 AND 1717203600
GROUP BY 
    s.APP_GROUP_ID,
    s.CAMPAIGN_ID,
    s.USER_ID,
    s.MESSAGE_VARIATION_ID,
    s.PLATFORM,
    s.AD_TRACKING_ENABLED

records.question: How many unique users started sessions each day within each app group between June 1, 2023, and June 7, 2023? Also show the app group ID and the start day of the session.
records.pred_sql:
SELECT 
    APP_GROUP_ID,
    DATE(TIMESTAMP 'epoch' + TIME * INTERVAL '1 second') AS session_start_day,
    COUNT(DISTINCT EXTERNAL_USER_ID) AS unique_users
FROM BRAZE_USER_EVENT_DEMO_DATASET.PUBLIC.USERS_BEHAVIORS_APP_SESSIONSTART_VIEW
WHERE TIME >= EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM TIMESTAMP '2023-06-01 00:00:00')
  AND TIME < EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM TIMESTAMP '2023-06-08 00:00:00')
GROUP BY APP_GROUP_ID, session_start_day
ORDER BY APP_GROUP_ID, session_start_day
